text.title: Solution Assessment Quality Rules
text.summary: Evidence requirements, sample-size rules, variance thresholds, and common failure modes.

rule.statement: Every metric verdict must cite at least one evidence URL with a retrieved_at timestamp. Never declare a metric "Met" based on memory, dashboard screenshots, or verbal reports — require a queryable primary source (analytics export, finance export, ticketing export).
rule.rationale: Agents hallucinate KPI values when data connectors are missing; humans misremember or report the number that supports the conclusion they want. Primary source citation is the only defense.

rule.statement: Every metric row must include sample size. A metric hit on n=12 is not a hit — it is statistically inconclusive. Require the original power calculation's minimum sample size before calling any metric "Met".
rule.rationale: Small samples produce variance that cannot be distinguished from noise; declaring success on underpowered data leads to wasted follow-on investment and erodes trust in future assessments.

rule.statement: Triangulate every business-value metric with at least two independent sources (e.g., analytics + finance, or analytics + survey). Single-source verdicts are fragile and gameable.
rule.rationale: A single metric source can be manipulated by changing how the query is run; two independent sources must agree before a verdict is reliable.

rule.statement: Use numeric variance bands for met/partial/not-met verdicts: variance ≥ 0% = Met; -15% to 0% = Partially Met; less than -15% = Not Met. Never use qualitative verdicts — "seems on track" is not a verdict.
rule.rationale: LLMs and humans both cluster verdicts in "partially met" when thresholds are not defined; numeric bands force a deterministic decision.

rule.statement: Mark requirements as "Deprecated" (not "Not Met") when the underlying business need has changed since the requirement was written. A binary Met/Not Met verdict on a stale requirement incorrectly penalizes the delivery team.
rule.rationale: Requirements written 9 months before delivery may reflect a different business context; forcing a Met/Not Met verdict on stale requirements produces misleading assessment reports.

antipattern.description: No baseline — cannot measure improvement. "Revenue went up after deployment" without a baseline is not a business value assessment.
antipattern.reason: Lock baselines before development starts using the same query and data source that will be used at assessment time.

antipattern.description: Survey-only assessment under-samples churners — surveys reach users who still use the solution; the ones who churned never answer.
antipattern.reason: Reconcile survey n with active user count; flag if response rate is below 30% or if churned users are absent from the panel.

antipattern.description: One-shot assessment — a single 30-day report misses the honeymoon effect; real friction shows at 90+ days.
antipattern.reason: Run assessments at 30, 60, and 90 days; compare the three reports. A metric that was on-track at 30 and at-risk at 60 is the real story.

antipattern.description: Assessor reports to the project sponsor whose bonus depends on the result — independence is compromised.
antipattern.reason: Route assessment through a different reporting chain or external reviewer. Independence is what gives the report weight at the steering committee.

text.reference: Python script for variance calculation and statistical significance
